title: Buttermilk layer cake
subtitle: A soft, tender white cake with vanilla buttercream
excerpt: Two 20 cm sponge layers made with buttermilk, sandwiched with vanilla buttercream. The buttermilk tenderises the crumb and reacts with the bicarbonate of soda for a slightly lighter texture than a standard butter cake. Twelve slices.
sourceNotes: Buttermilk layer cakes are documented in American cookery from the mid-nineteenth century onwards. Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896, Project Gutenberg), describes buttermilk and soda leavening for tender cakes, and the form appears throughout pre-1928 American baking guides and newspaper columns available through the Library of Congress digitised archives. The layered format with buttercream is attested in American domestic science publications of the early twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze layers before filling. Wrap individually, freeze up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature for 2 hours before assembling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Layers keep at room temperature for one day before filling. Assembled cake keeps in the fridge for 2 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 185°C. Two tins on one shelf; rotate halfway through if your oven runs hot at the back.

recipeTools.notes: For spreading the buttercream. A palette knife or the back of a spoon will do.

glossaryTerms.definition: Buttermilk is slightly acidic. In a cake batter that also contains bicarbonate of soda, the acid and alkali react to release carbon dioxide, which leavens the cake. The acidity also breaks down gluten strands, producing a more tender crumb than a cake made with whole milk. If you do not have buttermilk, add 15 ml of lemon juice or white wine vinegar to 300 ml of whole milk and leave for 5 minutes until it thickens slightly.

content.text: A buttermilk layer cake is an American classic: a pale, fine-crumbed sponge with a slight tang from the
, sandwiched with vanilla buttercream. Two 20 cm layers, filled and frosted. Twelve slices. The sponge layers need to cool completely before assembly.

Heat the oven to 175°C fan (185°C conventional). Grease and base-line two 20 cm sandwich tins. Beat the butter and sugar until pale and light, 3 to 4 minutes. Beat in the eggs one at a time, then add the vanilla. Sift the flour, baking powder, bicarbonate of soda, and salt together. Fold the flour and buttermilk into the butter mixture in alternating additions, starting and ending with flour. Do not overwork once the flour goes in.

Divide the batter evenly between the tins and bake for 25 to 30 minutes, until the tops spring back when pressed and a skewer comes out clean. Leave in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack and cool completely, at least 30 minutes, before filling.

Beat the softened butter until very pale and creamy, about 3 minutes. Add the sifted icing sugar a third at a time, beating well between each addition. Add the vanilla and enough milk to give a smooth, spreadable consistency. Spread half the buttercream on one cooled layer, place the second layer on top, and spread the remaining buttercream over the top.
